data.title: SEEM: A New Emerging Market ETF On The Block
data.description:
SEI Select Emerging Market Equity ETF Outlook

Emerging markets are poised to face a new set of headwinds following their strong outperformance in 2025. However, emerging markets were trading at only 13.5x forward earnings towards the end of February, and have since declined by around 8% on the back of new geopolitical tensions. The broader MSCI EM Index is favorable based on valuation and earnings growth, and still looks like a hold, even though emerging economies will be hit with higher inflation this year.

The SEI Select Emerging Market Equity ETF (SEEM) is an interesting vehicle to monitor. SEEM has slightly outperformed the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (EEM) since its inception in late 2024. SEEM is worth considering if you want to maintain passive exposure to emerging markets in this environment and don't want to bet on individual country/commodity-based narratives in the market.

The SEI Select Emerging Market Equity ETF has several unique characteristics relative to the MSCI Emerging Markets ETF, and it also has a lower management fee. SEEM's combined approach makes SEEM a slightly superior vehicle compared to EEM.

Emerging market macro risks, most notably inflation, have accelerated in recent weeks, and equities will likely continue to sell off in Q2 2026. Emerging markets also strongly rallied in 2025, so this quarter is not likely the best entry point. However, earnings growth for EM equities is very favorable, and SEEM looks like an acceptable vehicle for maintaining general exposure to emerging markets.

SEEM should continue its slight outperformance of the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF in 2026 and 2027.

SEI Select Emerging Market Equity ETF Overview

The SEI Select Emerging Market Equity ETF is a newer ETF that was listed in October 2024. SEEM tracks some of the top emerging market names in the market and utilizes quantitative techniques in its portfolio composition process. SEEM is an actively managed ETF with a 0.6% management fee that tracks the MSCI Emerging Markets Index.

The SEI Select Emerging Market Equity ETF targets a select list of around 230 companies in the MSCI Emerging Market Index, which has over 1,000 constituents. SEEM is also attractive in terms of valuation, and it traded at around a circa 19% discount to the MSCI EM Index as of the end of January 2026.

SEEM has a unique sector approach as it is underweight information technology (4 percentage points) and consumer discretionary (4 percentage points) stocks. Another unique aspect of SEEM is that it is slightly underweight emerging Asian stocks. Although this is not a huge difference, it has been enough to outperform the MSCI Emerging Markets Index by around 7 percentage points since its inception less than 2 years ago.

Relevant Emerging Market Trends to Note

Emerging markets will face a large number of headwinds in the coming months as tensions in the Middle East heat up. Inflation may rebound in select countries, and investors may continue to pull money out of emerging markets due to other economic risks in the market.

Fitch Ratings has issued a new warning for emerging markets amid rising energy prices and the Iran conflict, and noted that many emerging markets are heavily dependent on energy imports.

This setup is similar to 2022, when inflation ran hot in emerging markets following Russia's invasion of Ukraine. While inflation has improved substantially in global markets since 2022, current projections will likely be revised upwards in the coming months as they reflect the new risk of rising oil/food prices. The EM success narrative post-2022 was largely driven by emerging market Central Banks cutting inflation down to the lower single-digit levels.

Emerging market equities should continue to correct to reflect the new increased risk of inflation in March.

Global trade tensions will also likely decline, following the market's defiance of trade war concerns in 2025. The WTO has noted that it projects global trade to slow to 1.9% this year, down from 4.6% in 2025.

Allianz Trade recently compiled a large number of risks that could occur amid a delayed closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Only 2 months of disruptions could result in a circa 1pp increase in inflation in emerging markets, and many Asian countries may face shortages due to their high dependence on oil from the Middle East. Over 80% of the oil shipped from the Strait of Hormuz is bound for Asia.

President Trump extended his ultimatum yesterday for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

There is also a risk of a recession in select emerging markets. The IMF previously predicted that growth in the Asia Pacific region, which accounts for 60% of global growth, would decline to 4.1% this year. This may be revised downward further. The benefit of tracking the MSCI EM Index is that it has very strong exposure to Asian countries, which should have faster growth rates.

MSCI EM Index

This index also has strong exposure to developments in the semiconductor industry, as it invests in Taiwan and South Korea. The semiconductor industry previously grew by 22% in 2025, and Deloitte projects growth will reach 26% this year. SEEM still provides reasonable exposure to some of the IT-driven rallies in emerging markets.

SEEM also has slightly overweight exposure (6 percentage points) to regions like Latin America, emerging Europe, and emerging Africa. The Latin American region is intriguing because of its commodity exposure, while Africa and especially emerging Europe offer much lower valuations.

MSCI Emerging Europe Index

These differentiating points have led to slight outperformance in the market in the past 18 months.

Risks and Final Thoughts

Emerging markets have begun to pull back since the end of February, and this has largely been driven by corrections in dominant Asian emerging markets. Stock markets in India and Taiwan still trade at slightly over 20x forward earnings.

Q2 will likely be a bumpy ride for emerging markets, as investors continue to price in some of the geopolitical risks. However, emerging market equities were already attractively priced following these conflicts.

H2 2026 looks like a good time to add exposure to emerging markets, and SEEM will likely continue to outperform EEM in the long run.

data.title: UTG: A Strong Performer, But Not A Traditional Income Fund
data.description:
Intro

Utility income funds are supposed to be defensive in nature, offering steady monthly yields of between 6 and 8% and investing in reliable and boring utility companies that do not prefer to rock the boat. Reaves Utility Income Fund (UTG) has a $2.40 annual distribution and a current yield of about 6.2%. So UTG appears to fit that profile.

However, UTG paid out over $206 million in distribution last year but generated just $28 million in net investment income. That means only 14% of the payout came from steady income, and the rest came from realized gains and portfolio appreciation. This is not how traditional utility funds operate.

This is an important distinction for this fund. UTG’s performance has been driven by leveraged investment in stocks selected through a very specific criterion. That criterion is to build a portfolio tilted towards power producers specifically benefitting from AI-driven electricity demand.

That system has worked out well for UTG. In the past year, UTG has delivered a strong 27% NAV gain vs. 14.72% for the S&P Utilities Index; however, that specificity also means that UTG behaves less like a defensive utility income fund and more like a leveraged equity strategy. The strategy has worked - so far - distributing returns in an income-like format.

The Distribution Reality

86% of UTG’s distribution last year was sourced primarily from capital gains. Realized gains last year were $182.6 million, with another $558.4 million in unrealized appreciation in its portfolio. That means UTG is primarily relying on market performance, not portfolio income, to fund its payout. UTG has distributed over $1.7 billion to shareholders since its inception, showing how consistently this model has been executed

We have seen this with well-managed funds before: a stable distribution does not have to mean that the underlying income is stable. That’s where management comes into the picture. Its job is not to source returns from stable securities - that’s an impossible task to do consistently. The job is to have a stable distribution policy, even if the actual return comes from unstable equity market performance, as in the case of UTG.

UTG charges you a hefty 2.09% for this job, but let’s see how well it actually does it.

What UTG Actually Is

Traditional income funds generate most of their payout from recurring income. UTG is different - it is a leveraged equity portfolio focused on AI-driven utilities and infra, layered under a managed distribution policy.

Currently, UTG has around $3.52 billion in net assets and $875 million in debt, so the leverage comes to around 24.8% of net assets. Leverage increased from $650 million to $875 million YoY, which increases risk. Total investment exposure, at 124% of net assets, shows that leverage in practice. This leverage drives upsized returns as well as potential high losses.

Cash flow generated is modest, just around $93 million in dividend income during the year. After paying interest expense (~$37.7 million) and other fund expenses, this drops to roughly $28 million of net investment income.

Returns are, however, much stronger. The fund generated $741 million in total gains (realized + unrealized) over the same period. This gap between income and total return is exceptionally large in UTG and can be said to be its defining feature.

How Well It Has Worked

This system works very well when the equity market is strong. Currently, utilities, power producers, and infrastructure assets - all securities driven by AI - are strong. Fund NAV increased from $32.60 to $38.85 in one year. So UTG is getting realized gains, sustaining its distribution, and growing its NAV along the way. However, remove the realized gains, and there’s an 80% drop in income, in theory, which can lead to unsustainable capital usage to cover the payout. That may be an extreme scenario, but it highlights the dangers of equity dependency in an income portfolio.

UTG is mainly concentrated in utilities and infrastructure, but the composition is more aggressive than typical utility funds. The allocation breakdown is - ~33% multi-utilities, ~24.5% independent power and renewables, and ~19.4% electric utilities. The difference is in the nearly one-fourth allocation to independent power producers, which don’t behave like typical utilities at all. This is where UTG’s recent performance has come from.

Top holdings include Talen Energy, Vistra, and Constellation Energy, each of which have seen tremendous 52-week growth but with recent definitive slowdowns. Talen alone represents ~9.7% of total assets, and the top 10 holdings account for ~51% of the portfolio. Such stocks are directly exposed to wholesale electricity markets. These are not stocks you buy for dividend income - Talen pays none, while Vistra and Constellation pays ~.6% each - not defensive utility names in any sense. According to the fund’s own shareholder letter, high demand for electricity tied to AI and data centers has been a primary growth driver this fiscal year. A set of three factors - equity growth, leverage, and a supportive rate environment has contributed to this growth.

Peer Comparison: UTG vs. UTF vs. DNP

It is instructive to compare UTG to other names like Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF), its structurally closest peer, and DNP Select Income Fund (DNP), a contrasting fund. The yields are similar, but the underlying return structures are not.

Take UTF, the closest peer. Its distribution is 7.71%, per the latest factsheet. It is trading at an 8.36% discount to NAV, which is $27.39 versus market price of $25.10. UTF runs a leverage of ~30% of managed assets, while SEC yield is 2.3%.

That last number is critical. Just like UTG, UTF is funding its distribution not primarily from income, but from capital gains and return of capital. The difference with UTG is that UTF is more global, with only ~53% US exposure. It is also more infrastructure-oriented and has slightly higher leverage. It is basically a more diversified, higher-yield version of UTG, but with currency exposure. The expense ratio is 3.43%.

Now on to DNP, a security that represents the opposite end of the spectrum. Its current price is $9.91, its expense ratio is 2.53%, its yield is 7.87%, and it is trading at a small .4% discount to NAV. DNP has paid a stable dividend for many decades. Its yield remains stable because it buys regulated utilities and other income-generating assets. DNP also includes many fixed-income securities and is structurally designed for income stability, not growth.

This shows up in price performance, which is just ~3% in one year, compared to UTG’s 27%. In other words, DNP behaves like an income fund first and an equity fund second. That’s a key difference.

Another core difference is how these three trade relative to NAV. Currently, UTG trades near NAV, UTF trades at a significant discount, and DNP is trading at a small discount but historically trades at a persistent and often high premium.

The Hidden Risks

UTG has been a good performer, but there are several risks. The first one I will mention is that UTG is an equity fund disguised as income. There’s a perception risk here. Investors will read “utility” fund and think it is defensive - it is not. UTG’s distributions come primarily from capital gains, and it is also leveraged. That means the stability of your payout is directly linked to market conditions.

The second is the 25% leverage, which cuts both ways. In good times, it can enhance your gains; in bad times, it can worsen your losses. When NAV declines due to losses, it hinders the fund’s ability to generate future gains.

UTG’s portfolio is also highly sensitive to interest rates. On a direct level, there’s the $37.7 million in interest expenses. On an indirect level, higher rates pressure underlying assets, which hamper equity performance. UTG does not appear to have any hedge against higher rates, like interest rate swaps.

Conclusion

UTG is a well-managed fund with a decent yield, but it is not the defensive income fund it sometimes appears to be. UTG is a thematic equity fund whose distribution is driven by capital gains; returns are amplified by leverage, and its recent performance, at least, is based on AI-driven demand.

This has worked well in current environments, but it carries the risk that when the demand subsides, the fund may not have enough capital gains - let alone income - to fund its payout. Since the fund is also trading at NAV, this risk is only amplified. As such, I would be interested in UTG as a total-return strategy, but only when I can buy it at a significant discount to NAV.

data.title: Broadcom: Why I'm Buying And Holding This Stock Forever
data.description:
Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) shares have been in a bit of a downtrend the last few months as the top end of the AI sector has lost some momentum and the Iran war puts pressure on U.S. equities. Despite these near-term headwinds, Broadcom has remained a cash cow and will continue to dominate in the data center long-term.

In my last piece on AVGO back in March 2025, I discussed the company's strong prospects over the next 12 months as data center capex increases and ASICs make headway in the AI accelerator market. I rated the stock a Buy, and it has since appreciated by 73% relative to 15% for the S&P 500 (SPY). That article can be read here.

I didn't write on the stock for over a year as I focused on other areas of the tech world, but the more I look into the sprawling ecosystem of the modern economy, the more I keep coming back to Broadcom. And it's just getting started. Let's start with some quick context.

Despite turning in a highly successful last few quarters, AVGO has struggled to gain upward momentum in recent months as AI and macro fears have both come to the forefront. On the AI side, the market has started getting jittery when it comes to spending, as Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOG), Meta (META), and Amazon (AMZN) all have fallen precipitously (15%+) following recent earnings reports in which they committed to, cumulatively, more than $600 billion in capex for 2026.

On the macro side, the ongoing war in Iran is hurting the market as a whole, and many AI names remain at depressed levels even as tech has held up decently in the current risk-off environment. The main issue in focus is that higher oil prices have renewed inflationary fears, and odds of a rate hike are increasing, just weeks after a rate cut appeared inevitable.

It is with these two pressures that you can have a chart that looks like this:

And still get a stock that's down 5% over the last six months. Regardless of the reasons for this malaise, I think it presents a golden opportunity. The aforementioned AI capex is going ahead whether the market thinks the ROI is there or not, requiring massive amounts of compute and networking equipment, which Broadcom has in spades. This is already coming through in operating results.

For Q1 2026, the company reported revenue of $19.3 billion (+29% YoY/+7% QoQ), non-GAAP EPS of $2.05 (+28% YoY/+5% QoQ), and a record profit margin as network connectivity products, XPUs, and its newly revamped software business, courtesy of VMware, fired on all cylinders. Guidance was equally as impressive as management projected revenue of $22 billion (+47% YoY/+14% QoQ), including a 140% YoY jump in AI semiconductor revenue to $10.7 billion, or nearly half of total sales. Any fears that momentum was slowing have been dispelled by guidance showing the rate of YoY growth is actually increasing.

However, this outlook is small potatoes compared to this line from CEO Hock Tan:

Our visibility in 2027 has dramatically improved. Today, in fact, we have line of sight to achieve AI revenue from chips, just chips, in excess of $100 billion in 2027. We have also secured the supply chain required to achieve this.

Tan and Broadcom are starting to get a reputation for providing these grand AI projections in their earnings calls that often move the stock even more than the reported numbers. To achieve $100 billion in 2027 AI revenue, that would be a quarterly run rate of $25 billion, which represents 133% growth over the company's Q2 guidance for AI revenue and is higher than total Q1 revenue. Considering Broadcom has better visibility into AI infrastructure than almost anyone, it's hard to be skeptical about these numbers.

A Tale of Two Powerhouses

Most of this growth is being driven by two main product categories: compute (XPUs) and networking (Ethernet/DSPs). In the most recent quarter, XPUs made up 67% of AI revenue, leaving networking with 33%, as demand for both has exploded to meet the rapid pace of AI infrastructure build-outs.

XPUs specifically have found a real niche in hyperscale-level deployments as ASICs have grown in popularity as low-power, cheap alternatives to Nvidia's (NVDA) market-dominating GPUs, which offer greater flexibility but are much more expensive. When purchasing millions of processors, the math for many hyperscalers comes out in favor of the XPU over the GPU, especially when they're as pricey as Nvidia's.

I have been expecting this shift for a while now and think we have finally reached an inflection point where the balance of power is beginning to show signs of shifting away from Nvidia (you can read my article on the topic here). Hyperscalers designing their own custom accelerators is the future of the market, and Broadcom, with its dominant position in high-end ASICs, will reap those rewards.

Despite the company's dominance in ASICs, it is even more dominant when it comes to the unsung hero of its AI business: networking. This category includes Tomahawk Ethernet switches, Jericho routers, and a variety of optical components like digital signal processors (DSPs), all of which are vital to the operation of any data center. Even though networking is the little brother now, Broadcom expects the segment to grow from 33% to 40% of AI revenues as the increasing size of data centers means increased demand for these chips.

Similar to XPUs, this is an area where Broadcom is bringing the fight to Nvidia. The company has made major inroads with its Ethernet switches, which are fast and flexible and avoid the vendor lock-in that Nvidia enforces with its proprietary NVLink (intra-rack) and InfiniBand (inter-rack) switches. In fact, to counter Broadcom's gains, Nvidia has been pushing its own Ethernet switches but is currently months behind, as the Spectrum-X1600, which is its answer to Broadcom's Tomahawk 6, won't release until late 2026. With how much shorter generation life cycles are in the AI world, being a few months behind means potentially losing billions of dollars in business.

Investor Takeaway

It is this commitment to engineering excellence and identifying trends before they become mainstream and lucrative that makes Broadcom a smart investment -- when a company takes on Nvidia and wins, I pay attention.

Broadcom's AI footholds are strong and growing, preparing it for a future with ever-higher transmission speeds, data volumes, and compute. The business of interconnecting devices is one that the company has excelled at for a long time, and it has put itself in the driver's seat for AI and whatever other secular trends may come down the road.

In the short term, I expect AVGO to mostly follow the broader market as this macroeconomic shock plays out. Even the blowout earnings weren't enough to generate any upward momentum, so anyone hoping for a quick buck should probably sit this one out for a bit.

However, I think those with a long-term investing horizon should view current price levels, though they might seem expensive as the stock hovers around a forward P/E of 30, as a buying opportunity. This is a generational behemoth that will continue to generate massive cash flows ($8 billion, or 41% of revenue, in Q1) and capitalize on growing data needs with its best-in-class chips and wide moat for many years and decades to come.

I am upgrading AVGO from a Buy to a Strong Buy and adding it to my list of Stocks To Own Forever ("STOF").

Thanks for reading!

data.title: How Risky Are BDCs Really?
data.description:
Understanding how BDCs lend, manage credit risk, and navigate interest rate environments can help investors better evaluate their role in a diversified income portfolio.

How BDCs Manage Credit Risk

While BDCs operate in a higher-yielding segment of the credit market, their portfolios are typically structured with risk management in mind. First, many BDCs focus on senior secured loans. These loans sit higher in a borrower’s capital structure and are backed by collateral, which can improve recovery prospects in the event of financial stress.

Second, BDC portfolios are usually diversified across industries and borrowers. This diversification helps reduce the impact of any single credit event. Lastly, experienced BDC managers rely on active underwriting and ongoing monitoring. Because many of these loans are privately negotiated, managers often maintain close relationships with borrowers and can respond more quickly to changes in company performance.

Interest Rates and Floating Rate Income

Interest rate exposure is another factor investors often consider when evaluating BDCs. A large portion of BDC loans are structured as floating-rate instruments. The interest payments on these loans adjust with changes in benchmark rates such as SOFR. When interest rates rise, the income generated by these loans may increase. When rates decline, income from floating-rate loans may fall. That said, lower borrowing costs can ease financial pressure on borrowers, which may help support credit quality. In that sense, interest rate changes can have offsetting effects within a BDC portfolio.

Dividend Stability and Income Generation

One reason BDCs are popular with income-focused investors is their distribution structure. BDCs are required to distribute most of their taxable income to shareholders, which supports the relatively high yields associated with the asset class. Many BDCs also maintain dividend coverage ratios, which provide an indication of whether portfolio income comfortably supports the dividend. Some maintain spillover income, representing earnings retained from previous periods that can help support distributions during weaker quarters. These features can help smooth income volatility and provide greater visibility into dividend sustainability.

Market Volatility vs. Portfolio Fundamentals

Listed BDCs trade publicly on equity markets, which means their share prices can fluctuate based on broader investor sentiment. During periods of market stress, BDC stocks may trade at discounts to their net asset value, or NAV. These discounts can sometimes reflect short-term concerns rather than changes in the underlying loan portfolio.

For long-term investors, such dislocations can present potential opportunities. Buying BDCs at a discount to NAV can allow investors to access the income stream of the underlying portfolio at a more attractive valuation.

Investing in BDCs

BDCs are not risk-free investments. Their focus on middle-market lending means they operate in a segment of the credit market that carries higher risk than traditional investment-grade bonds.

However, the asset class also incorporates structural features designed to manage that risk. Senior secured lending, diversification, floating rate structures, and active credit management all play a role.

For investors seeking income and exposure to private credit markets, BDCs may represent a differentiated source of yield. As always, understanding the underlying portfolio structure and credit strategy is key when evaluating the opportunity. The VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) offers diversified exposure to publicly traded BDCs. Rather than picking individual BDCs, which carry concentration risk tied to specific managers and borrower pools, BIZD tracks the MVIS US Business Development Companies Index across the industry’s largest names.

Important Disclosures

The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned are unknown. An investor cannot invest directly in an index. Returns reflect past performance and do not guarantee future results. Results reflect the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains, if any. Certain indices may take into account withholding taxes. Index returns do not represent Fund returns. The Index does not charge management fees or brokerage expenses, nor does the Index lend securities, and no revenues from securities lending were added to the performance shown.

Business Development Companies (BDCs) generally invest in less mature U.S. private companies or thinly traded U.S. public companies which involve greater risk than well-established publicly-traded companies. While the BDCs that comprise the Index are expected to generate income in the form of dividends, certain BDCs during certain periods of time may not generate such income. The Fund will indirectly bear its proportionate share of any management fees and other operating expenses incurred by the BDCs and of any performance-based or incentive fees payable by the BDCs in which it invests, in addition to the expenses paid by the Fund. A BDC’s incentive fee may be very high, vary from year to year and be payable even if the value of the BDC’s portfolio declines in a given time period. Incentive fees may create an incentive for a BDC’s manager to make investments that are risky or more speculative than would be the case in the absence of such compensation arrangements and may also encourage the BDC’s manager to use leverage to increase the return on the BDC’s investments. data.title: AMLP: For High-Yield Investors Seeking Defensive Energy Exposure
data.description:
Introduction

After covering all of the largest names in the midstream industry and, more particularly, MLPs, I want to dive into how investors can get a basket exposure to all of those great companies without the hassle of K-1s, as well as individual risk. In that sense, I will be reviewing the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) and why I believe that this will be a strong investment.

AMLP Specificities

The Alerian MLP ETF tracks the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index, which captures the largest and most liquid midstream MLPs in North America. With roughly $12.13B in assets, this is the undisputed heavyweight in the MLP ETF space.

The fund holds just 13 names, and that concentration is by design. The top five holdings are currently Sunoco (SUN), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Western Midstream Partners (WES), Energy Transfer (ET), and Plains All American Pipeline (PAA), with each carrying over 12% of the portfolio. You can directly assess that I’ve been bullish on most of these names and how they are prone to go higher in the future.

To resume this, every midstream company in AMLP has activities that generally provide stable cash flows that are highly independent of volatile energy prices thanks to their tollbooth businesses.

Currently, the ETF is on the more expensive side, with 0.85% of annual expenses compared to the 0.5% broad ETF average, or even MLPA’s (MLPA) 0.46%. Though it is interesting to mention that AMLP has historically outperformed MLPA despite the fee gap, and it offers the advantage of liquidity compared to its smaller peers.

Distribution Yield

The fund is paying a distribution yield of 7.5%, which is simply incredible considering the current average of the MLP sector and the broader market at 1.15%. But what excites me more would be the growth track record. After the massive 21% distribution cut during the COVID pandemic, when the entire energy complex was in freefall, AMLP has grown its payout steadily for five consecutive years. The growth rate is also impressive, at a 5-year CAGR of 6.56, especially considering that the ETF’s yield is at 7.5%.

The reason the distribution keeps growing comes down to what is happening in the industry. The MLPs in this portfolio have undergone a genuine transformation over the past 5 years. They went from capital-destruction machines, funding growth with equity dilution and leverage, to now disciplined free cash flow generators. That is why I rate them so high in my various coverages, as they are becoming some of the most efficient companies out there. Distribution coverage across the large-cap MLP space now averages roughly 1.7, showcasing quite an enormous buffer.

The Permian Is Not Slowing Down

Many believe that US production is set to stagnate or decline, but at the current rate, the Permian is actually debunking this theory, mainly in NGLs, as EPD is predicting that the Basin will be responsible for around 85% of the growth in the industry through 2030. For AMLP’s holdings, this matters directly. More Permian volumes mean more gathering, more processing, and more pipeline throughput. Meaning that as volumes increase in the Permian, revenue will increase for all MLPs that have exposure through gathering, processing, and else. For example, MPLX is betting 90% of its growth capital on natural gas and NGLs in the Permian, while the Blackcomb Pipeline, which will be able to transport up to 2.5Bcf/d is expected to come online in H2 of this year.

Moreover, these projects are generally backed by LT contracts and anchor shippers, putting a touch of responsibility on the capital involved. ET has just done one of the better examples of this new era as it canceled the Lake Charles LNG project to focus on instantly accretive projects.

NGL Exports

Talking about NGL, the US is currently expanding its ability to export ethane, which is on track to see 2 years of 15%+ growth through 2027. EPD, again, is the single largest NGL exporter in the world, which makes it prone to capitalizing on this, and is currently the 4th largest holding.

This is not a cyclical phenomenon. International petrochemical crackers are being built with the explicit assumption that cheap US NGLs will feed them for decades. Meaning that the demand is set to continue to be enormous with all of the steps required to ship printing money for MLPs based on the toll booth model.

AI Demand

This is the tailwind that frankly surprises me. The AI infrastructure buildout is creating enormous new demand for natural gas-fired power generation, and the midstream companies that move natural gas are direct beneficiaries. Companies like ET have surged on exactly this thesis, as the market is starting to price in the reality that AI data centers will need a lot of gas, and someone has to move it through pipes.

The EIA forecasts total US natural gas production reaching nearly 120 Bcf/d in 2026, yet another record, with a large chunk of incremental demand coming from power generation for data centers. For AMLP’s portfolio of gas-focused midstream operators, this is a demand pull that simply did not exist 3 years ago.

How AMLP Compares To Its Peers

There is currently a basket of MLP-focused ETFs available to investors, and I believe that it is important to structurally differentiate between them.

The main drawback would be the expense ratio, that’s for sure. AMLP charges nearly double what MLPA and MLPX (MLPX) charge at 0.85%, which will eat into compounded returns. So why, despite covering the same market segment, do I prefer AMLP?

First, AMLP trades over 1.2MM shares per day compared to roughly 155K for MLPA. This means that recurring buyers will benefit from a better spread as well as being able to get in or out of a large position more easily than thinly traded peers.

Second, despite the higher expense ratio, AMLP has historically outperformed MLPA on both a one-year and three-year basis. The index methodology and rebalancing mechanics produce a slightly different exposure that has significantly rewarded investors.

Third, AMLP offers the highest distribution yield in the peer group at 7.5%, compared to 7.4% for MLPA and a much lower 3.4% for MLPX. If income is the primary objective, and for most MLP investors, it is, AMLP delivers the most quarterly cash.

The Capital Discipline Transformation

MLPs are highly different than what they were just a decade ago, yet many on the market believe that their operations are still the same, which is why I believe there is still a gap to exploit, in which AMLP offers coverage.

The large MLPs in AMLP’s portfolio have collectively shifted from growth-at-all-costs to a return-of-capital model, with leverage ratios decreasing all across the board. Meanwhile, distribution coverage has continued to drastically increase, offering a relative safety net. FCF after distributions is positive, giving management teams the flexibility to pursue bolt-on acquisitions, buy back units, or continue deleveraging.

EPD, for example, has grown its distribution for 27 consecutive years while maintaining an A-minus credit rating. Energy Transfer, one of my largest holdings, has gone from being the poster child of MLP recklessness to generating consistent free cash flow and reducing its leverage below 4.0. WES has delivered aggressive distribution growth while keeping coverage well above 1.5.

Risks To Consider

Though AMLP allows investors to bypass K-1 forms, it is structured as a C-Corp, unlike regular ETFs, because it holds more than 25% of its assets in MLPs. This actually requires the fund to pay corporate income taxes on its income and capital gains. That is why there might be such variance with the benchmark.

The high fee is also a pain, as over long time periods, which I’m sure investors are pursuing, a higher fee can meaningfully eat into the compound of returns. Moreover, distributions from AMLP are classified as returns of capital, which can result in higher capital gains liabilities in the future.

Conclusion

To be fair, the Alerian MLP ETF represents a great entry for high-yielding investors that want exposure to the more defensive side of the energy industry in North America that currently has a lot of tailwinds going for it, such as the continued dominance of the Permian, the expansion of NGL exports, and the energy-hungry nature of AI. At the current yield, which is simply gigantic and avoids K-1 forms, I can only rate this ETF as a Strong Buy.

data.title: Nvidia: The Market Is Wrong; It Has To Be
data.description:
There are seasons in the market where I've noticed good (even great) earnings don't push stocks continually higher. Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) is a really good recent example with its massive and historic quarter and guide. Yet, it's down nearly 13% since then. But NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) is also a good candidate for great earnings, but not great stock performance. In the past, I've looked at it simply: the market has reached its peak for rewarding good fundamentals. But I would still scratch my head as to "why now?" It wasn't until I realized fundamentals don't drive markets; sentiment, and more specifically, mass market human psychology, does. It turns out sentiment leads and fundamentals lag. Furthermore, it explains these seemingly seasonal times when the markets just "go down to go down" or, more directly, "go down on good news."

There are narratives trying to explain the stock performance away, but they fall flat when scrutinized. Trying to find the needle of a bearish point in a haystack of great business fundamentals is a wasted effort because it leads nowhere; it merely scratches an intellectual itch while your long position sees profits erode. At the end of the day, I don't care about the reason per se as much as I care about whether my trades and investments are profitable and getting more profitable.

After a life's worth of watching the markets and 12 years writing about them, I've realized the simple truth: the only thing that matters is what the share price says. It doesn't matter if I think the market is "wrong," the number didn't change. I can either find the truth of how the stock price got to where it is, or I can remain arrogant and say, "I know I'm right; everyone else (read: the market) is wrong."

This article can serve as an example of how to either fight against the forces of the market or take the first step in understanding how to align yourself and your thinking with the market's mind to know where it's thinking about going next.

Defeating The Bear Thesis With Great Numbers

Let's start with the earnings numbers, because they really show the dichotomy of business performance versus stock performance. Then I'll get into the reasons for the stock's drop.

The company has resumed its revenue growth acceleration after seeing it dip during the Chinese product ban early last year and expects this post-China acceleration to continue in the current quarter. It reported $68.13B for FQ4 '26 and guided for $78B for FQ1 '27. This was above Wall Street's estimates of $66.23B and $72.03B, respectively. What's impressive is the year-over-year acceleration, not just the sequential.

At the same time, gross margins have done what management expected them to do: dip when ramping up new generations of products and return to prior highs once new products take over revenue. In the end, the bearish talking point about competitors coming after those juicy gross margins has never come to fruition. NVIDIA's margins are as strong as they've ever been, and it's now been roughly four years since the AI race started.

The path Nvidia is on now still has quite the runway. While I don't see the original $500B guidance issued last year or the more recently issued $1T guidance issued this year as eye-popping once you do the math and consider the timeline of the revenue realization, as some would believe, there's no indication of a slowdown in Nvidia's business.

There Are Narratives, Of Course

Nonetheless, to scratch the intellectual itch of trying to make sense of a stock down, we have narratives to account for it. But, as I'll get to, these don't help you in a predictive manner.

The most prominent narrative to come out of the report was that Nvidia's customers are upping CapEx, which is good for it (more spending on chips), but its cash flows are being pressured because of it—bad for the sustainability of CapEx. This is a very legitimate concern that is the basis of why I wrote the AI returns article in January. If these hyperscalers can't find a return, even just revenue, let alone profit, then there are serious implications coming out of 2026 with the AI industry not being sustainable as a business.

But Jensen Huang doesn't seem at all pessimistic. He believes cash flows won't be pinched, perhaps at all, when 2026 is all said and done.

I am confident in [our customers'] cash flow growing. And the reason for that is very simple. We have now seen the inflection of agentic AI and the usefulness of agents across the world and enterprises everywhere. You're seeing incredible compute demand because of it. In this new world of AI, compute is revenue. Without compute, there's no way to generate tokens. Without tokens, there's no way to grow revenues. So in this new world of AI, compute equals revenues... I am certain at this point that we are at the inflection point... and we're generating profitable tokens that are productive for customers and profitable for the cloud service providers... And AI to have -- in order to generate tokens, you need compute capacity. And that translates directly to growth, and that translates directly to revenues.

- Jensen Huang, CEO, Nvidia's FQ4 '26 Earnings Call Q&A (emphasis added).

Is this possible? Very much so; 2026 is still early. Currently, guidance is for compressed free cash flow from most of the big tech companies. Can that outlook change? Absolutely. Jensen believes it will come about from agentic AI. Regardless, something more productive and sellable must come along and provide the return, or at least enough to push the needle further.

Getting To The Bottom Of This

But even with this confidence, the stock has not responded positively. And, as I alluded to a few moments ago, these are hindsight conclusions, unhelpful for getting ahead of being gapped down on great earnings.

Before I get into the sentiment aspect of why this is all happening, you need to be aware that this article isn't the starting point for my analysis. In December, I was already seeing what was to play out today in March. It was the same basic idea: great fundamentals and business execution, but a stock not able to make any further headway higher. This article is making you aware that we are in the middle of further declines, not getting to the end of it just yet.

So, how exactly did I see today's action months ago? The simple answer is I'm analyzing sentiment on the stock chart. And because of the structure we're in, it has been building to the selloff we're seeing now.

Without trying to talk over your head, I'll point out what prompted me to become very cautious on Nvidia recently.

The basic idea is understanding whether a new rally was starting off the November lows or whether this was the second leg of three of a larger correction. The red boxed-in area is what showed me this was the latter.

This was forming the B wave of the A-B-C wave correction. Aside from the actual analysis of the structure I employed on the chart, I determined it was a B wave by some observable features, even to the naked eye, of the attributes of a B wave, which include choppy, overlapping, and difficult-to-follow patterns.

The outcome of this B wave is the continuation of the trend we saw in November—down. Ironically, the end of the B wave happened on earnings, which then set us off into the C wave we're now in.

The question you're likely asking now is, "Where does it go from here then?"

This is determined by the Fibonacci levels, showing the relationship between waves A and B to point to C. Concurrently, there's also the lower-degree wave relationship of the five waves that constitute the C wave. Sometimes these don't always agree on price, so I have to have a base case and a more extended case (at least on one wave degree).

If I look at just the A-B-C target, this is pointing toward the 100% extension at the $162.60 area. If, instead, I look at the five-wave move in the C wave, it points to something quite a bit lower, ultimately pointing toward $138.

My process is to take it one step at a time and see how the stock reacts at the levels as they approach. If it breaks down decisively below the 100% extension of waves A and B, then my lower-degree look at the C wave is likely what's playing out. If it holds the 100% extension and we see a more meaningful bounce, then we either could have completed the correction or have just completed the first leg of an even larger correction set to play out over the next year or two. In either scenario of the 100% extension becoming support, we could have completed the C wave and then see a larger bounce over the coming months.

These parameters and decision points narrow us down into the near and medium term. Once they do, it makes the targets more probable and clear. Nonetheless, I'm still looking lower in the near term, as we're still above the 100% extension.

Look At The Correct Signals

Getting back to the idea that the fundamentals look great, but sentiment is still not ready to bottom, is the market wrong?

Last I checked, I can't choose the price I get to buy or sell at any given moment. I can wait for the price I want, but I could be waiting for a long, long time, maybe never. Thus, I decide to see what the market is telling me about where it's going. Even if it's not pinpoint accurate, as the market can move to and fro on its schedule and in its own way, the overall idea of seeing a corrective structure, especially a B wave, building and building, I know a C wave is coming after it. If I would rather not ride it down, I can exit before the C wave starts, and if I want to go long, I can wait for a better opportunity lower still coming.

Are fundamentals meaningless? No, I don't believe so, as bankrupt companies aren't likely to rally like a profitable one. But the common denominator is that fundamentals don't lead; they lag sentiment. This is why we end up with good fundamentals still going on while sentiment already shifts.

Nevertheless, in the end, why be the guy who says the market is wrong when it fixes nothing?

data.title: Columbia Cornerstone Growth Fund Q4 2025 Quarterly Portfolio Recap
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from Columbia Cornerstone Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

U.S. equities posted a gain of 2.41% in the fourth quarter, as measured by the Russell 1000 Index. The positive three-month return was primarily a function of the same factors that propelled stocks over the full year: namely, better-than-expected economic growth, robust corporate earnings results and inflation that largely remained below an annualized rate of 3%. The U.S. Federal Reserve continued to ease policy, cutting rates by a quarter point at its meetings in October and December and announcing an end to the multi-year effort to reduce the size of its balance sheet. Stocks also remained supported by ongoing excitement surrounding the artificial intelligence (AI) theme, albeit with a brief stretch of concern in November that AI-related equities were in a "bubble."

Notably, the quarter was characterized by a broadening of leadership away from the mega-cap technology stocks that had been key drivers of market performance for most of the year, as value stocks outpaced their growth counterparts. At the style level, value stocks rose 3.81% — based on the Russell 1000 Value Index — and outpaced the 1.12% gain for the Russell 1000 Growth Index.

Top holdings (% of net assets): as of December 31, 2025

NVIDIA (NVDA) 13.03
Apple (AAPL) 10.72
Microsoft (MSFT) 10.68
Amazon.com (AMZN) 6.17
Alphabet-A (GOOGL) 5.37
Broadcom (AVGO) 4.79
Meta Platforms (META) 4.41
Eli Lilly & Company (LLY) 3.76
Visa (V) 2.90
Netflix (NFLX) 2.11
Top holdings exclude short-term holdings and cash, if applicable. Fund holdings are as of the date given, are subject to change at any time, and are not recommendations to buy or sell any security.

Top five contributors - Effect on return (%) as of December 31, 2025

Alphabet-A 1.22
Eli Lilly & Company 1.01
Apple 0.69
Alphabet-C (GOOG) 0.58
Exact Sciences (EXAS) 0.43

Top five detractors - Effect on return (%) as of December 31, 2025

Microsoft -0.74
Oracle (ORCL) -0.57
Netflix -0.51
Meta Platforms -0.47
Uber Technologies (UBER) -0.24

Quarterly portfolio recap

Within health care, positive contributions were led by Exact Sciences, as the provider of a home screening test for colon cancer was acquired by Abbott Laboratories (ABT) at a significant premium to its trading price. Eli Lilly was another standout performer, posting strong earnings driven by its dominant position in obesity treatments, while providing favorable updates on an oral weight-loss drug in development.

Within consumer discretionary, travel website operator Expedia (EXPE) outperformed on strong earnings growth that exceeded expectations, driven by positive booking and profitability metrics, while raising guidance. The company is well positioned through its operational improvements to benefit from a strong travel backdrop. Amazon reported a strong quarter that handily beat expectations, supported by results in its cloud computing and AI hyperscaler businesses. In addition, advertising revenue from its online shopping platform was higher than expected.

Sector weights (%): fund vs. benchmark as of December 31, 2025

Within financials, the overweight exposure to large banks, including Citigroup (C), Goldman Sachs Group (GS) and Bank of America (BAC) proved beneficial. Sentiment around these stocks benefited from strong earnings reports driven by increased capital markets activity and strong trading results. In addition, an improving regulatory outlook should support continued capital returns to shareholders.

Alphabet highlighted contributions within communication services. The provider of the Google search engine had been viewed as a potential “AI loser”, but has flipped that narrative based on the impressive performance of its Gemini generative AI assistant and share gains for its large language model used to generate predictive text. In addition, Alphabet reported strong advertising revenue from its Google search and YouTube streaming segments.

On the downside, within information technology, shares of ServiceNow (NOW) declined in the quarter on news that the workplace productivity platform provider planned to acquire cybersecurity company Armis. The market appeared to react to the short-term impact of the deal on earnings and potential challenges around integration. We believe these concerns are overblown, and we view the acquisition as strategically sound with respect to ServiceNow's existing capabilities and growth opportunities. Shares of cybersecurity company Palo Alto Networks (PANW) also moved lower on news of an agreement to acquire Chronosphere, a provider of "observability" software used to diagnose IT system issues. While the deal comes with some execution risk and near-term pressure on free cash flow, we believe the acquisition fits well with Palo Alto's strategy and opens up a new avenue of growth. Database software and enterprise application provider, Oracle was another laggard. The company posted results in keeping with expectations and reported a strong order backlog. However, investors have become concerned about the company's rising capital expenditures and increasing debt financing to support the company's significant backlog of cloud infrastructure contracts, as well as customer concentration risk, given its massive long-term deal to support OpenAI's data centers. We view the growth trajectory for Oracle as attractive, and we expect that improving margins will support higher free cash flow than anticipated and help ease concerns around financing.

Within industrials, shares of ride sharing and meal delivery company Uber technologies moved lower after the company reported strong bookings but lower-than-expected profitability, with management saying that it is deliberately moderating the pace of margin expansion in order to continue reinvesting in the business. In addition, reports that Waymo, which partners with Uber in some cities, was independently expanding its autonomous ride-hailing service in four new cities weighed on the stock. Power management company Eaton (ETN) underperformed after providing guidance that was disappointing relative to high expectations. Eaton remains well positioned to benefit from the buildout of AI data centers, which require intense power use and advanced technology to dissipate heat. We expect the company to experience accelerated growth as it integrates recent acquisitions and recent capacity additions begin to contribute to revenue.

While selection was generally positive within communication services, social media company Meta Platform was a laggard, with shares pulling back on the announcement of plans to significantly increase spending on efforts to develop artificial general intelligence and integrate it across its platforms. Shares of Netflix declined as the streaming service reported an earnings miss primarily driven by a one-time tax-related charge. The stock then dipped again on news of a proposed acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) raised questions about Netflix's strategy, given the company has historically preferred to invest in generating its own original content.
